documents.title: Initech Systems Q3 2024 Earnings Call
documents.content:
Operator: Good afternoon. Welcome to Initech Systems' third quarter 2024 earnings conference call. All lines are in listen-only mode. I will now turn the call over to Karen Liu, Head of Investor Relations.

Karen Liu -- Head of IR: Thank you, operator. Good afternoon, everyone. With me today are Raj Patel, our CEO, and Lisa Thompson, our CFO. Today's remarks contain forward-looking statements. Actual results may differ materially. We will refer to non-GAAP measures; reconciliations are in today's press release.

Raj Patel -- CEO: Thanks, Karen. Good afternoon. I'm pleased to report another strong quarter as organizations continue to prioritize cybersecurity spending despite the challenging macro environment. Q3 revenue was $310 million, up 28% year over year and 5% sequentially. This represents our eighth consecutive quarter of accelerating growth.

Raj Patel -- CEO: ARR reached $1.25 billion at quarter-end, up 30% year over year. Net dollar retention was 125%, up from 122% last quarter, reflecting strong expansion within our installed base. We added 150 new logos in Q3, our strongest quarter ever for new customer acquisition. Total customers now exceed 3,800.

Raj Patel -- CEO: On product, Initech Shield 5.0, our next-generation XDR platform, is seeing rapid adoption since its launch in July. Shield 5.0 integrates endpoint, network, cloud, and identity security into a single platform with AI-powered threat detection. Customers deploying Shield 5.0 are seeing a 60% reduction in false positives and 45% faster mean time to respond compared to our previous generation.

Raj Patel -- CEO: The government and public sector remains a strength. Government revenue was $78 million in Q3, or 25% of total revenue, up 35% year over year. We secured two new large DoD contracts and expanded with three civilian agencies. We're pursuing FedRAMP High authorization, which we expect to achieve by mid-2025.

Raj Patel -- CEO: Internationally, revenue was $93 million, or 30% of total, up 32% year over year. EMEA grew 30%, led by the UK, Germany, and the Nordics. APAC grew 38%, driven by Australia and Japan. We opened our first office in the Middle East, in Dubai, to serve the growing Gulf region market.

Lisa Thompson -- CFO: Thank you, Raj. Let me cover the financial details. Non-GAAP operating margin was 12%, up 400 basis points year over year. This is a significant milestone—our first quarter above 10% operating margin. Gross margin was 82%, up 100 basis points sequentially, reflecting the continued shift to subscription and improved cloud infrastructure efficiency.

Lisa Thompson -- CFO: Subscription revenue was $279 million, or 90% of total revenue, up from 85% a year ago. Professional services revenue was $31 million, or 10% of total.

Lisa Thompson -- CFO: Free cash flow was $65 million in Q3, a 21% free cash flow margin. We ended the quarter with $850 million in cash and investments. CapEx was $18 million. We did not repurchase stock in Q3; our board authorized a $200 million buyback program in September, which we'll begin executing in Q4.

Lisa Thompson -- CFO: DSO was 52 days, improved from 58 days a year ago. We're working to bring this down further by shifting more customers to annual prepaid contracts.

Lisa Thompson -- CFO: Headcount was 2,100 at quarter-end, up 20% year over year. R&D was 28% of revenue, reflecting our heavy investment in the Shield 5.0 platform and AI capabilities. Sales and marketing was 35% of revenue. G&A was 7%.

Lisa Thompson -- CFO: For guidance, we're raising our full-year revenue outlook to $1.20 billion to $1.22 billion, from $1.15 billion to $1.18 billion previously. For Q4, we expect revenue of $325 million to $335 million, representing 25% to 29% growth. We're raising full-year operating margin guidance to 11% to 12%, from 9% to 10% previously.

Operator: We will now begin the question-and-answer session.

Operator: The first question comes from Brian O'Connell with Piper Sandler.

Brian O'Connell -- Piper Sandler: Thanks. Raj, the net retention of 125% is impressive. What's driving the expansion? Is it Shield 5.0 upgrades or additional modules?

Raj Patel -- CEO: It's both, Brian. About half the expansion is customers upgrading to Shield 5.0, which comes with a higher per-endpoint price. The other half is module attach—our cloud security and identity modules are the biggest cross-sell drivers. We're also seeing customers protect more endpoints as they consolidate vendors. Many of our customers have gone from protecting 5,000 endpoints to over 20,000.

Brian O'Connell -- Piper Sandler: And on competition, CrowdStrike and Palo Alto are both pushing consolidation. How do you win against them?

Raj Patel -- CEO: Great question. Against CrowdStrike, we differentiate on government and regulated industries—we have deeper compliance certifications and on-premises deployment options they don't. Against Palo Alto, our advantage is that we're purpose-built for XDR, not bolted on from a firewall heritage. Our detection efficacy is independently validated as best-in-class by MITRE ATT&CK evaluations. In Q3, our win rate against CrowdStrike was 52% and against Palo Alto was 58%.

Operator: The next question comes from Nancy Chen with Wells Fargo.

Nancy Chen -- Wells Fargo: Hi, thanks. Lisa, on the margin trajectory, 12% operating margin is a big step up. Where does this go over the next 12 months?

Lisa Thompson -- CFO: We're targeting 15% or higher non-GAAP operating margin by Q3 2025. The drivers are revenue scale, improving gross margins as subscription grows, and moderating hiring growth. R&D at 28% of revenue will come down slightly as we lap the Shield 5.0 build. But we'll continue to invest aggressively in AI—we have 200 ML engineers and plan to add 50 more by mid-2025.

Nancy Chen -- Wells Fargo: And on the $200 million buyback, what's the expected cadence?

Lisa Thompson -- CFO: We plan to execute the buyback ratably over the next 12 to 18 months, with some flexibility to be opportunistic. Given where our stock is trading, we see meaningful value. We don't expect the buyback to impact our ability to pursue M&A; we have plenty of capacity for both.

Operator: The next question comes from Jake Morrison with Deutsche Bank.

Jake Morrison -- Deutsche Bank: Thanks. Raj, on the Middle East expansion, what's the addressable market there and how quickly can it ramp?

Raj Patel -- CEO: The Gulf region cybersecurity market is growing over 20% annually, driven by digital transformation initiatives like Saudi Vision 2030 and UAE's national cybersecurity strategy. We've already signed two large enterprises in the UAE and are in late-stage conversations with three government entities. We expect the Middle East to contribute $15 million to $20 million in revenue in fiscal 2025. It's early but high-growth.

Jake Morrison -- Deutsche Bank: And on the threat landscape, the recent increase in nation-state attacks—are you seeing that translate into urgency from customers?

Raj Patel -- CEO: Absolutely. The escalation in nation-state activity, particularly from groups affiliated with Russia, China, and North Korea, is driving urgency at the board level. Our pipeline is up 40% year over year, and deal cycles have shortened by about two weeks on average. CISOs are consolidating onto fewer, better platforms, which plays to our strength. The board-level urgency is especially acute in critical infrastructure, healthcare, and financial services.

Operator: The last question comes from Amanda Foster with Morgan Stanley.

Amanda Foster -- Morgan Stanley: Thanks. Quick one on customer churn. You mentioned 150 new logos. What does gross churn look like?

Raj Patel -- CEO: Our gross dollar churn rate remains below 5% annually, which is best-in-class for cybersecurity. Logo churn is slightly higher because we do lose some SMB customers, but dollar churn is low because our enterprise customers are very sticky. When you combine a sub-5% churn with 125% net retention, the economics of our installed base are very compelling.

Amanda Foster -- Morgan Stanley: And any thoughts on M&A? There are a lot of point-solution vendors out there.

Raj Patel -- CEO: We're actively evaluating opportunities, particularly in cloud-native application security and operational technology security. These are adjacencies where our customers are asking for help and where we can leverage the Shield platform. We have the balance sheet to do $100 million to $200 million deals. Nothing to announce today, but you should expect us to be more acquisitive over the next 12 months.

Karen Liu -- Head of IR: Thank you all for joining us today. We'll be at the Wells Fargo TMT conference next week and Barclays in December. Have a good evening.

Operator: This concludes Initech Systems' third quarter 2024 earnings call. You may now disconnect.
